coredata.dc:title: Numerical and experimental characterisation of high energy density 21700 lithium-ion battery fires
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Institution of Chemical Engineers.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Institution of Chemical Engineers.
coredata.dc:creator: Vendra, Chandra M.R.
Shelke, Ashish V.
Buston, Jonathan E.H.
Gill, Jason
Howard, Daniel
Read, Elliott
Abaza, Ahmed
Cooper, Brian
Wen, Jennifer X.
coredata.dc:description: High energy density lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are well suited for electrical vehicle applications to facilitate extended driving range. However, the associated fire hazards are of concern. Insight is required to aid the development of protective and mitigation measures. The present study is focused on 4.8 Ah 21700 cylindrical LiNixCoyMnzO (NMC) LIBs at 100% state of charge (SOC) with the aim to develop a viable predictive tool for simulating LIB fires, quantifying the heat release rate and temperature evolution during LIB thermal runaway (TR). To aid the model development and provide input parameters, thermal abuse tests were conducted in extended volume accelerating rate calorimetry (EV-ARC) and cone calorimetry. Some cells were instrumented with inserted temperature probe to facilitate in-situ measurements of both cell internal and surface temperatures. The mean peak values of the heat release rate, cell surface and internal temperatures were experimentally found to be 3.6 kW, 753 °C and 1080 °C, respectively. An analytical model has been developed to predict cell LIB internal pressure evolution following vent opening. The model uses the measured cell internal temperature and EV-ARC canister pressure as input data. Its predictions serve as boundary condition in the three-dimensional computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulation of TR induced fire using opensource code OpenFOAM. The predicted transient heat release rate compare favourably with the measurements in the cone calorimetry tests. Predictions have also been conducted for an open cluster to assess the likelihood of TR propagation in the absence of cell side rupture. The present modelling approach can serve as a useful tool to assess the thermal and environment hazards of TR induced fires and aid design optimisation of mitigation measures in enclosed cell clusters/modules.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 21700 Lithium-ion battery
Cell internal pressure and temperature evolution, Venting of flammable gases
Fire simulation
Computational Fluid dynamics (CFD)
Safety

Numerical setup for the fire simulation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The experimental setup for the cone calorimetry tests: (a) distribution box equipment, (b) support mesh, (c）LIB and heater and (d）thermocouples (e) LIB instrumentation.
Measurements for (a) HRR and (b–d) temperatures in the cone calorimeter.
Pressure canister of the EV-ARC.
The EV-ARC measured chamber pressures and LIB surface temperature.
The EV-ARC measured battery Internal and surface temperature.
(a-b) CFD domain for single LIB fire simulations, (c) LIB vent cap before (d) LIB vent gap after the test.
EV-ARC pressure canister pressure and LIB internal temperature during TR.
The cell internal pressure evolution predicted by the analytical model.
Mesh sensitivity study of flame temperature at centreline (30 cm).
Experimentally captured images of the three stages in TR evolution: (a) soft venting, (b) sparking (c) jet fire.
The transient variation of the LIB flame.
The experimentally measured heat release rate and cell surface temperature at mid-height in the cone calorimeter.
Comparison between the predicted and measured heat release rate.
The predicted flame centreline temperature at different heights.
Open LIB cluster geometry: 1 – Inlet, 2 – LIB and 3 – plastic casing.
The temperature variations predicted on the adjacent LIB top for an open cluster.
LIB specifications.
The HRR and THR in the three tests.
The vent gas compositions measured in the three tests.
The Arrhenius model parameters for Eq. (7).It is still under review. Happy for you to remove this one

head.ce:title: Numerical and experimental characterisation of high energy density 21700 lithium-ion battery fires

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: High energy density lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are well suited for electrical vehicle applications to facilitate extended driving range. However, the associated fire hazards are of concern. Insight is required to aid the development of protective and mitigation measures. The present study is focused on 4.8 Ah 21700 cylindrical LiNixCoyMnzO (NMC) LIBs at 100% state of charge (SOC) with the aim to develop a viable predictive tool for simulating LIB fires, quantifying the heat release rate and temperature evolution during LIB thermal runaway (TR). To aid the model development and provide input parameters, thermal abuse tests were conducted in extended volume accelerating rate calorimetry (EV-ARC) and cone calorimetry. Some cells were instrumented with inserted temperature probe to facilitate in-situ measurements of both cell internal and surface temperatures. The mean peak values of the heat release rate, cell surface and internal temperatures were experimentally found to be 3.6 kW, 753 °C and 1080 °C, respectively. An analytical model has been developed to predict cell LIB internal pressure evolution following vent opening. The model uses the measured cell internal temperature and EV-ARC canister pressure as input data. Its predictions serve as boundary condition in the three-dimensional computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulation of TR induced fire using opensource code OpenFOAM. The predicted transient heat release rate compare favourably with the measurements in the cone calorimetry tests. Predictions have also been conducted for an open cluster to assess the likelihood of TR propagation in the absence of cell side rupture. The present modelling approach can serve as a useful tool to assess the thermal and environment hazards of TR induced fires and aid design optimisation of mitigation measures in enclosed cell clusters/modules.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Cell internal pressure and temperature evolution, Venting of flammable gases

ce:def-description.ce:para: Pre-exponential factor in the Arrhenius model
Total heat generation per unit mass (kJ/kg)
number of moles (mol) or reaction order in the Arrhenius model
Heat from combustion of cell generated oxygen (kJ)
specific rate of dissipation of kinetic energy

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIB) are increasingly used in electric vehicles, consumer electronics and stationary energy storage devices. However, the individual components of LIBs are flammable in nature. High energy density batteries offer extended operating range/duration or increased power output, but their contents are more densely packed. The potential fire hazards associated with these batteries are of major safety concern (Lisbona and Snee, 2011; Waldmann et al., 2020; Lin et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021). LIBs are mostly safe within their designed usage environment. However, accidents occur when LIBs are exposed to abuse conditions (Quintiere, 2021; Sun et al., 2021), which can be divided into three categories: (1) thermal abuse due to overheating and fire, (2) electrical abuse due to overcharge, overdischarge and external/internal short circuit, and (3) mechanical abuse incurred by crash, penetration, and bend. These abuse conditions can trigger rapid exothermic reactions inside the LIB, which generate excessive heat and gases, leading to increases in LIB internal temperature and pressure. Once the designed critical pressure for the LIB safety vent is reached, the vent opens to release the generated gases. This is quickly followed by thermal runaway (TR), ejecting more gases generated from the abuse reactions. The exact compositions of the vented gases vary with LIB chemistry but typically consist of carbon dioxide (CO2), carbon monoxide (CO), hydrogen (H2), and some hydrocarbons. The flammable gases can auto ignite or be ignited by the hot sparks ejected from the LIB (Chen et al., 2020), resulting in fire and even explosion. Numerous incidents associated with LIB fire and explosion have been reported involving consumer electronic devices, electric vehicles, and aircraft (Liu et al., 2021). It is hence of importance to gain insight of the flaming behaviour of high energy density LIBs to aid the development of protective measures and inform fire rescue services.

As summarised in a recent review (Sun et al., 2021), a large body of literature exists for 18650 cylindrical LIBs. In comparison, relatively few investigators have addressed the safety related issues of 21700 LIBs (Chen et al., 2020, 2021; Surampudi et al., 2021). The authors’ team (Chen et al., 2020) experimentally investigated 5 Ah 21700 LIBs under uniform heating with a flexible heater and used established correlations between heat release rate (HRR) and the mean flame height of turbulent jet flames to estimate the thermal hazards of the resulting fire. They also conducted further tests for the same LIB with flexible and nichrome wire heaters (Chen et al., 2021). Some others reported modelling of 21700 LIBs when subjected to nail penetration or thermal abuse (Surampudi et al., 2021; Kim et al., 2021; Johnsplass et al., 2017). A brief review will be given about the previous modelling efforts in the following.

While significant progress has been achieved in the application of Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) techniques to process safety (Ruiqing Shen et al., 2020), detailed CFD modelling of LIB fires are scarce in the literature due to constraints in determining both experimentally and theoretically precise boundary conditions for transient LIB fires. Kim et al. (2021) used Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) approach with ANSYS commercial software to simulate 18650 LIB at various states-of-charge (SOC) (100%, 50%, 25%) to study detailed flow and thermal behaviour during venting. They developed a numerical model for the cell internal pressure during venting and gas-phase dynamics of 18650 LIB undergoing TR. Two-dimensional (2-D) numerical simulations were conducted but there was no experimental data for validation. They also found that LIBs with higher SOCs produce more heat and gas during TR owing to higher mass and concentrations of reacting gases, leading to higher cell internal pressures (Kim et al., 2021). Li et al. (2021) conducted 3-D simulations of hot gases venting through the safety vent during TR. Their results highlighted the potential to investigate the effects of jet structure and turbulence levels on the subsequent combustion and heat transfer in TR propagation.Johnsplass et al. (2017) developed a 1-D predictive approach based on Cantera, an open-source software for simulating thermodynamic states and flames. They proposed a laminar burning velocity correlation as a function of concentration, pressure and temperature for the vent gaseous mixture. However, it was not clear how laminar flame speed, which was often used in combustion models for premixed flames, was used to model the fires, which are essentially non-premixed with very different features. Surampudi et al. (2021) conducted 3D-CFD simulations with commercial code ANSYS Fluent to study the electrochemical and thermal behaviour of 21700 NCA LIB. A four-equation thermal abuse model along with the internal short circuit model were developed. The predicted temperatures were compared with the measurements in the authors’ single nail penetration tests. They also extended the model to simulate TR propagation in cell clusters without considering the venting of hot gases and resulting fire. Similar modelling of LIB TR was also performed using COMSOL Multiphysics software (Melcher et al., 2016; C.J. Wang et al., 2021). Most investigators only considered heat transfer by conduction and neglected convective and radiative heat transfer, which have considerable influences on the propagation of TR in cell clusters. Such approximation is acceptable when the adjacent LIBs are in proximity. However, even in such scenarios, as air is a poor conductor of heat, convective heat transfer still has some influence. When flames are established, thermal radiation plays a dominating role (Sacadura, 2005). Various attempts have been reported to predict TR propagation in cell clusters by coupling electrochemical and thermal abuse models (Wenhe Wang et al., 2021) without incorporating the effects of the resulting fire. The thermal hazards from fire can significantly alter the TR propagation pattern and accelerate the rate of TR propagation. More recently, Kong et al. (2022) modified open source CFD code to predict the cell temperature and internal pressure evolution under thermal abuse, venting and subsequent combustion of 18650 NCA LIBs. A lumped model was developed to predict generation of gases due to the abuse reactions and calculate the internal pressure from literature data of cell internal void space. The predicted cell internal pressure revealed the existence of two peaks with the 1st peak at the time of vent valve opening and 2nd peak at the onset of TR. The peak cell internal pressures were found to be 1.63, 3.01, 4.03 bar for 25%, 50% and 75% SOC, respectively. which indicates higher SOCs cause a more violent ejection during thermal runaway. The subsequent 2-D fire simulations used empirically estimated jet velocity and literature data of mean gas compositions as boundary conditions, neglecting the effect of thermal radiation.

Strictly speaking, numerical simulations of TR resulted fires require the transient vent gas compositions and flow rates as boundary conditions. However, their measurements remain a big challenge. Sturk et al. (2019) performed in-line measurement of CO2, unburnt hydrocarbon and hydrogen fluoride (HF) during thermal abuse tests in inert atmosphere. The packs of five LFP/NMC/LMO pouch LIBs were used. The major species were found to vary with LIB chemistry and the SOC levels. Yuan et al. (2020) experimentally identified the major gas components by using gas chromatograph to analyse the samples collected from ARC tests. Baird et al. (2020) summarised experimental analysis from 20 tests and found that the major species include H2, CO, CO2 and hydrocarbons. The fractions of H2 and CO were found to increase with SOC while the fraction of CO2 decreased with the increase of SOC for some LIB chemistries, resulting in increase in fire hazards due to less inert gas in the vent gas mixture. Similar gas analysis was also performed by Golubkov et al. (2014) for the vent gases of LCO/NMC/LFP. While each LIB type showed a unique gas composition, the main components identified were H2 and CO2 with significant amount of CO plus smaller fractions of methane (CH4), ethylene (C2H4), and ethane (C2H6). Due to lack of measurements for time-resolved vent gas compositions, the overall measurements of the major vented species concentrations were adopted as uniform vent gas compositions in almost the previous CFD simulations (Sun et al., 2021; Kong et al., 2022). It should be noted that no gas composition measurements have been reported for 21700 LIBs, and none of the published vent models and CFD modelling of TR addressed this type of LIB.

One of the important parameters for defining fire hazard is the heat release rate (HRR), which gives information about the intensity at which the fire releases energy in the form of heat. The quantification of the HRR is essential for predicting the propagation of TR to neighbouring cells. Different experimental methods such as thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) (Sanjay, 2021), differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) (Wang and Huang, 2020), accelerating rate calorimetry (ARC) (Yuan et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2019) were employed to study the energy release during TR while cone/in-situ calorimetry (Vijay Somandepalli, 2014; Mao et al., 2020) were used to quantify HRR from the resulting fire.

In the present study, the TR induced venting and fire hazards are numerically simulated for 4.8 Ah 21700 cylindrical NMC LIBs at 100% SOC. An analytical model has been developed to capture the cell internal pressure evolution from vent opening onwards and determine the initial/boundary conditions for the 3-D CFD simulations of the resulting fire. Experimental investigations have been conducted in extended volume ARC (EV-ARC) and cone colorimeters to provide input parameters for the vent model and boundary conditions for the CFD simulations. Comparison is made between the predicted and measured energy release. A key innovation of the present study is the coupling between the predictions of TR, vent gas and fire simulations as well as the extension to cell clusters. It is also novel to use the measured cell internal temperature and canister pressure to evaluate the cell internal pressure, which is both challenging to obtain either experimentally or analytically without experimental support to identify the void space which varies with cell types.

The simulations were performed using open source CFD code OpenFOAM (Weller et al., 1998; Magnussen, 2005). The code has been previously validated for a wide range of jet and pool fires by the authors’ group for both single and multiple component fuels. The RANS approach is used to solve the compressible flow with the two-equation model k-ω shear stress transport turbulence model of Menter (1994). Combustion is treated using the Eddy Dissipation Concept (EDC) (Magnussen, 2005), which assumes that chemical reactions occur in the fine structures and these structures are in the order of the Kolmogorov scales. The formulations are listed in the Supplementary material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical model for determining the LIB internal pressure
ce:section.ce:para:
The internal pressure of LIB is an important parameter which affects the vent gas flow rate. It is also needed as boundary conditions for the fire simulation. To develop a simplified model for the cell internal pressure, the LIB and EV-ARC pressure canister are considered as the system and surrounding. Measurements were conducted for LIB surface temperature, canister temperature and its internal temperature. The ejected gases were collected in the pressure canister filled with inert atmosphere of argon or nitrogen. Applying the Dalton’s law of partial pressure, the chamber pressure can be written as the sum of the partial pressures of the initial inert gas and the partial pressure generated due to the releases of the vent gases from the cell.
(1)
P
c

P
ar
+
P
vent

Differentiating Eq. (1) with respect to time

Using ideal gas law, the above equation can be expanded to
(2)

The partial pressure of the vent gas is affected by the change in the chamber temperature and the rate of change of the moles of the vent gas into the chamber, whereas the partial pressure of the initial argon gas is only affected by the change in the temperature of the chamber. In Eq. (2), the rate of change of the canister pressure

The first term in the right hand side of Eq. (6) requires modelling to estimate the amount of gas generated during self-heating phase of the LIB while the second term can be evaluated from the ARC canister pressure measurements through the ideal gas law. Using the analogy of an auto catalytic reaction rate, the rate of thermal abuse and heat generation can be estimated with a one-step global expression (Chen et al., 2020) for the self-heating phase of the cell as
(7)

where, ‘α’ is the degree of reaction progress, ‘

is the total heat generation for the unit mass. The rate of conversion of LIB mass is therefore given by Eq. (7). The rate of conversion of the LIB mass into heat can be considered analogues to the rate of generation of the gases due to the thermal abuse and can be modelled as in Eq. (8)
(8)

where, ‘ x ’ represents the volatile moles fraction and is a modelling parameter. Substituting Eq. (8) into Eq. (6) and subsequently into Eq. (5), the cell internal pressure (
P
int
) during TR evolution can be obtained.

Experiments were conducted to characterise the TR characteristics of the present 21700 NMC LIB at 100% SOC and provide essential measurements for the vent model and energy release. These include three repeated cone calorimetry tests, EV-ARC tests with pressure measurements in the canister and two LIBs with inserted internal temperature probes. Brief descriptions of individual experiments are presented in this section with the detailed specifications of the tested LIB listed in Table 1.

Cone calorimetry tests were conducted to characterise the energy released during TR. Prior to the tests, fresh batteries were firstly discharged and then charged with the same constant-current-constant-voltage (CC-CV) to 100% SOC. A schematic of the experimental setup used in this work is shown in Fig. 1. The LIB was placed horizontally upon a supporting mesh made of iron wire. Three K-type thermocouples of 1 mm in diameter, maximum range of 1300 °C, and measurement precision of 0.1 °C were attached to each LIB as shown in Fig. 1(d) at top (T1), middle (T2), and bottom (T3) section to measure the surface temperatures. The distribution box was used to regulate the power of the cylindrical heater shown in Fig. 1(a).

As shown in Fig. 1(c), a cylindrical heater with the same physical dimension as the LIB (21 mm in diameter and 70 mm in height) and a power rating of 200 W was used. To some extent, this mimics the situation of one failed LIB transferring heat to a neighbouring LIB. The fire generated gases were extracted through an exhaust fan. The instrument LIB along with the cylindrical heater was place horizontally on the support mesh Fig. 1(e). An electrical scale with the resolution of 0.01 g was used to measure the LIB mass loss during test.

The measured heat release rates in the repeated tests are shown in Fig. 2(a) and temperature measurements in Fig. 2(b–d). The plotted HRR values were calculated based on the oxygen consumption principle from the oxygen measurements. As oxygen was generated by the decomposition of the cathode at elevated temperatures. This would result in measured oxygen consumption rate to be smaller than the actual value. To avoid the problem, oxygen generated from the decomposition of the cathode material was calculated to correct the HRR. The total heat release (THR) ‘Qt’ can be expressed as:
(9)
Q
t
= Q +
Q
s
where ‘Q’ is the heat measured from the calorimeter, ‘Qs’ is the heat from the combustion with self-generated oxygen which is released from cathode materials. The energy of oxygen consumed per unit mass is 13.1 kJ according to the principle of oxygen consumption method. The peak HRR and the total energy release obtained by integrating the HRR curves are shown in Table 2 for the three tests under heating power of 200 W. There are some variations in the magnitudes of both HRR and THR, which can we attributed to the fact that no two LIB fires behave the same. While there are some common trends for a given LIB type and components, considerable variations occur from one test to another. For example, a relatively big difference is also noted between the " QS" in Tests 1 and 3. Additionally, the temperature in Test 2 experienced a sudden drop. Although the LIB and thermocouples were initially securely fastened to the workbench and LIB sides in the cone calorimeter tests, the violent movement of the LIB during TR evolution rendered some thermocouples to become dislodged from the intended location, resulting in lower temperature measurements. As the temperature measurements between Tests 1 and 2 have relatively better repeatability, their average are used to deduce the input conditions for the CFD simulations.

EV-ARC tests were conducted to measure the battery surface temperature, pressure of the canister and the overall compositions of the vent gases. The LIB was tested in a canister arrangement to measure any volume of gas given off during its decomposition as shown in Fig. 3.

The “heat-wait-seek” approach was used to provide information about the temperature at which the battery becomes unsafe as well as the quantity of thermal energy released by the battery in decomposition and the time it takes for the battery to fully decompose. The tests simulate a “worst-case” adiabatic environment that is similar to the conditions in the middle of a large battery pack where heat from a LIB cannot easily escape to the surroundings. The measured surface temperature and the chamber pressure are shown in Fig. 4. The peak temperature of 786 °C and peak chamber pressure of 7.12 bar were measured in one of the EV-ARC tests. The volume of the pressure canister is 4.3 L. This is equivalent to a vent gas volume at standard temperature and pressure conditions of 273.15 K and 105 Pa to be around 9717 cm3.

The vent gases were collected from the canister connected to the EV-ARC to analyse the compositions of the major gas species. In Tests 1 and 2, the vented gases were collected in the canister using nitrogen (N2) as an inert gas while Test 3 used argon (Ar) as inert. This approach gives the overall amounts of gases generated rather than the transient values which need can be obtained from in-situ detection. The collected gases were sent for further analysis to quantify the compositions. Table 3 shows the major vent gas compositions for the three tests with some variations in the measurements between them, especially the first test gave higher percentage for CO2 while the other two tests were similar. The second tests measured higher percentage of CO. There are less variations in the measured percentages of H2 and hydrocarbon gases. Given these uncertainties, the average values from the three tests are used in the CFD simulations as input to approximate the transient gas compositions during TR evolution.

To facilitate measurements of LIB internal temperature during TR, a temperature probe was inserted into the battery central void space avoiding contacts with any of the battery components. The LIB was then re-sealed to ensure leak proof even under elevated temperatures in the thermal abuse tests. The internal temperatures measured in the two repeated tests are plotted in Fig. 5. The peak internal temperatures were 1109.3 °C and 1051.5 °C. The corresponding peak surface temperatures were 712 °C and 793.5 °C, respectively. Clearly, the temperature inside the LIB where the abuse reactions take place is much higher than that on the surface, where heat is dissipated to the environment. The measured temperature difference for the 21700 LIB is approximately ~ 300 °C in the radial direction during their peak values.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Numerical setup for the fire simulation
ce:section.ce:para: The computational domain for the fire simulation is shown in Fig. 6(a) and (b). This region represents the space above a single LIB excluding the cell itself. The mesh was generated using the ‘blockMesh’ utility in OpenFOAM. The grids were refined and concentrated towards the centre and the mesh resolution reduces gradually away from the centre in the radial direction. The mesh distribution was kept uniform in the axial direction along the length of the domain. The vent opening at the LIB anode face is defined as the inlet boundary at the bottom centre face and the remaining domain boundaries were specified as outflow patches. The initial turbulence boundary conditions is set as 3% of the mean velocity at vent opening. Transient simulation is conducted with a courant number of 0.3 and initial timestep of 10−5 s.

The vent cap of the 21700 LIB considered in the present study has three openings of ~1.5 mm each. During the tests, the vent cap was found to become completely dislodged and the central opening was around 10 mm in diameter as shown in Fig. 6(d), indicating the potential of improving vent design to improve LIB safety (Li et al., 2022). In some of the tests, the jelly roll also ejected out, leaving the central vent opening of greater than 10 mm. The present simulation only considered the gases ejecta, neglecting the eruption of the jelly roll, the modelling of which would not only requires the track of the internal pressure but also finite element analysis to capture the jelly roll deformation and collapsing. The vent opening size was assumed to be 10 mm following the experimental observation.

A range of boundary and initial conditions are required to carry out the fire simulations. These include the fuel composition and its flow rate as well as the temperature and pressure of the released gases. Measurement of the cell internal pressure evolution is particularly challenging. Various researchers including our group have tried to make such measurements by inserting pressure probes into the densely packed 21700 LIBs, but so far no successful measurements have been reported for this type of cell. Although few such experiments have been performed for 18650 LIBs but no two reported values are in close proximity to one another. In the present study, the analytical model described in Section 2.2 is used to estimate the evolution of the cell internal pressure. The experimental measurements described in Sections 3.1–3.3 were used to provide the necessary input to the analytical model. As shown in Table 3, the mean overall composition of the vent gases calculated from the three sets of measurements is used to represent the ejected gases. These are H2 – 22%, O2 – 0.38%, CO – 17.70%, CO2 – 42.33%, C2H4 – 1.46%, C3H8 – 1.3% and CH4 – 14.8%, giving a mixture molecular weight of 27.79 g mole−1. Similar vent gas components were identified as major species in the experimental measurements for 18650 NMC LIBs at 100% SOC by Baird et al. (2020). Strictly speaking, the vent gas compositions and temperature vary with time during the venting processes. Because of the lack of measurements for real time variations, adopting an experimental measured overall vent gas composition is a common practice adopted by most modellers (Chen et al., 2020, 2021; Kim et al., 2021; Mao et al., 2021).

As discussed in Section 2.1, the single step infinitely fast chemistry is used to determine the reaction rate in the EDC combustion model. The single step was developed based on global irreversible chemical reaction as a surrogate fuel for the vent gas mixture. The thermophysical properties of the surrogate fuel were evaluated from the sum of the mole fraction ratios of the considered individual vent gas properties.

The analytical model described in Section 2.2, is used to estimate the evolution of the internal pressure using the EV-ARC measured LIB internal temperature, chamber pressure and temperature as shown in Fig. 7(a–c). Substituting the experimental measurements and canister volume of 0.0043
m
3
into Eq. (2), the rate of change of moles of the vent gas in the ARC pressure canister is evaluated as
d
n
vent
dt
, which is equivalent to the rate of release of the vent gases in moles in Fig. 7(d). The peak number of moles for the vent gases released is 0.53 during TR and then decreased as those with low volatility condensed due to rapid temperature drop following venting of the hot gases into the canister.

The volatile moles fraction ‘ x ’ in Eq. (8) was set to 0.112 to provide the best estimate of the peak HRR values of 3.5 kW as averaged from the cone calorimeter measurements for Tests 1 and 2 as shown in Fig. 2(a). The coefficient of discharge in Eq. (5),
C
d
, depends on the shape of the vent opening and on the pressure ratio of discharge. To keep the model simple, a fixed value of 0.9 was assumed. The predicted LIB internal pressure evolution with time by the analytical model is shown in Fig. 8.

It should be clarified that the analytical model predicts the cell internal pressure from the moment of vent opening. This was also the time when the canister started collecting the vented gases (Fig. 7a). This is unlike other published measurement or predictions which considered the pressure evolution in the canister or inside the cell for the whole duration of TR evolution (Mao et al., 2021; Ziebert; Lei et al., 2017).

The analytical model predicts the internal pressure from the moment of vent opening. It assumes that the vent opens when the internal pressure reaches the critical vent opening pressure, which is set by the manufacturer as 27.9 bar as shown in Table 1. The Arrhenius model parameters shown in Table 4 are model constants for Eq. (7), obtained by log curve fitting the EV-ARC LIB temperatures profiles following the same steps described in our previous work (Chen et al., 2021). The predicted cell internal pressure evolution in Fig. 8 shows two pressure peaks. The first peak is the critical vent opening pressure and the second peak is due to TR, which is predicted to be around 4.56 bars for the present 21700 NMC LIB. Although no experimental measurements are available for direct comparison, the occurrence of the second peak during TR is in line with the experimental measurements for NCA 18650 LIB by Ziebert and Lei et al. (2017), which detected the second internal pressure peak to be around 5 bar. Similar value and trend were also predicted for 18650 LIB by the recent prediction of Kong et al. (2022). However, Kim et al. (2021) and Mao et al. (2021) predicted the second internal pressure peak to be around 2.5 bar and 1.056 bar, respectively for 18650 LIB. during the TR. The existence of the second internal pressure peak at TR was also experimentally found by H. Wang et al. (2021) for 50 Ah prismatic cells and Zhao et al. (2020) who measured the canister pressure for 18650 LIB. However, the inconsistency in the measured/predicted values of the second internal pressure peaks by different authors for 18650 LIBs suggests that the actual predicted and measured values of the internal pressure peak at TR should be considered with caution for both 18650 and 21700 LIBs in the lack of validation.

As the LIB internal and external ambient pressure ratio is higher than the critical value for choked flows, which is typically 0.58 for diatomic gases. To avoid simulating the shock-laden flow at the nozzle exit of an under expanded jet flow, the pseudo diameter approach is used. It is physics-based semi-empirical model derived from the conservation of mass and momentum to calculate the jet conditions across the pseudo diameter as sonic velocity at ambient pressure. In the present study, the pseudo diameter approach of Birch et al. (1984) is adopted to evaluate the vent gas velocity at the pseudo inlet which is the opened vent. The ARC canister temperature and pressure are considered as the ambient condition. The gases were assumed to be venting at the experimentally measured cell internal temperature. The calculated pseudo diameter, which serves as the inlet boundary of the fire, changes with time due to the continuous variation of the LIB internal pressure. Once the internal pressure reaches equilibrium with the ambient pressure, the Pseudo diameter is the same as the vent opening diameter.

The transient CFD predictions are presented for the temperature at various heights above the vent as well as the HRR and temperature at the top of the adjacent cell to evaluate the likelihood of TR propagation in LIB clusters.

The RANS CFD approach is known to be sensitive to mesh resolution. To establish that the numerical predictions are independent of the mesh size, preliminary simulations were performed with three different mesh sizes, i.e. 3.5, 4.5 and 5.0 million. The predicted flame temperature variations with time at 30 cm height are compared in Fig. 9. It can be discerned that the differences in the predictions of the medium and fine meshes are very small. Therefore, the final simulations were performed with the medium resolution as a compromise between computational efficiency and accuracy.

The underlying complex chemical and physical processes in TR involve decomposition of the electrode materials, burning of the electrolyte as well as ejection of sparks and flammable gases and the subsequent fire (Chen et al., 2020). The whole process can be divided into soft venting, initial sparking and flaming.

The initial ejected sparks mostly consist of molten anode and cathode materials. During the sparking stage, no sustained flames are observed in the experiments. Towards the end of the sparking stage, the ejected gases were ignited and flaming lasts throughout the remaining TR process. The flaming started as a momentum dominating jet fires and gradually transited to buoyancy driven (Chen et al., 2020). Fig. 10 shows images extracted from the high-speed video recordings of the LIB thermal abuse experiment conducted using the facility at the Science Division of the Health and Safety Executive under heating with a side patch heater to trigger TR following the same procedure as described in our previous paper (Chen et al., 2020). The ejected sparks can act as ignition sources for the vented flammable gases. But it might also be possible that these gases auto ignited as the measured internal temperatures, as shown in Fig. 7(c), which are well above the auto-ignition temperature of the flammable gaseous constituents. The authors’ group previously analysed (Chen et al., 2020) the high speed video files to establish these distinct phases mentioned afore and proposed a simplified approach to estimate the heat release rate from the resulting jets fires using established correlations for jet fire heights (Heskestad, 1999).

Fig. 11 presents snapshots of the predicted fire evolutions for qualitative comparison, showing similar trends with the experimentally observed images presented in Fig. 10. Some quantitative comparison between the predicted and measured HRR will be discussed in the next sub-section.

The average values of the measured HRR and mid-height cell surface temperature from Tests 1 & 2 in the cone calorimeter are plotted in Fig. 12. Two important observations can be made. Firstly, there is a slight time delay between the LIB surface temperature and HRR peaks. The duration of the stable LIB fire can be considered as having the same time span as the HRR curve. The slight time lag between the temperature peak and the HRR peak can be attributed to the ignition delay from the initial sparking stage. Although the hot sparks carry energy and can transfer it to the surrounding cells, such energy release cannot be captured by the oxygen consumption method. This is indeed a limitation in using such method to quantify the energy release induced by LIB TR. In addition, some deviations can also occur due to oxygen generation in the decomposition of the cathode/anode/electrolyte materials itself, which is corrected in the current experimental analysis and found to be negligible by previous research. Huang et al. (2021) (Chander and Ray, 2005) compared the theoretical heat release rate from normalised electrolyte molecules with those obtained from the cone calorimeter tests and found that the HRR curves predicted by the two approaches had similar values and trends. This implies that the oxygen generated during the early stages of the decomposition reactions were ejected out before combustion even started.

The predicted and measured HRR in the cone calorimeter are compared in Fig. 13. The predictions match well with the measurements up to the peak HRR when the volatile moles fraction
" x
” was set to 0.1125 in the analytical model for the internal pressure evolution. The predicted HRR deviate more from the measurements after the peak. This does illustrate the limitations of the present simulation but is of relatively less concern as the main purpose of the simulation was to capture the heat release rate during TR resulted fire at its peak. Quantitative comparison between predicted and measured LIB fires will be hard to achieve. Firstly, as discussed earlier no two LIB fires are the same. There is a great degree of stochasticity in the cell internal structure change due to material deformation and decomposition. Side ruptures can also occur and the actual size of the opening on the cell top is not limited to the vents and vary from cell to cell as shown in Fig. 6(d). It is also challenging to match the timing in the prediction with that of the video frames in the experiments. Even though it is almost impossible to quantitatively validate CFD predictions of LIB fires to gain further confidence about the accuracy the predictions, it is viable to use CFD predictive tools validated by similar fire scenarios (Magnussen, 2005) to establish the trends of the fire evolutions and the potential hazards establishing the worst-case scenario to inform emergency services and development of mitigation measures.

The centreline temperatures along the flame length are shown in Fig. 14. High temperature zone was concentrated towards the central section of the flame length at different heights. The relatively high temperatures from flaming indicate when considering fire effects in cell clusters/modules, the effects of such fires impinging on the casing should be considered to assess whether the fire would melt/penetrate the casing.

Radiative heat transfer plays a very important role in fires and its hazard for process safety. In many situations, radiation is the dominant mode of heat transfer in the vicinity of a fire source, especially for large-scale fires. For enclosed LIB clusters, radiative heat transfer is an important contributing factor for heating abuse of the neighbouring cells. To evaluate the effect of different radiative heat transfer treatment, predictions were performed using both the P1 and the more accurate finite volume based discrete ordinate method (fvDOM). The P1 is a simplified approach but computationally more efficient than the more accurate fvDOM. In Fig. 15, the two sets of predictions are compared in terms of the radiative heat transfer on an adjacent cell in an open configuration, which are approximately 22 mm from the cell on fire i.e. with 1 mm air gap between the cells. The gas radiation properties were treated with grey gas assumption for the unburnt fuel as well as combustion products H2O and CO2. For simplicity, soot contribution to radiation and ray scattering factors were neglected.

The predicted LIB surface temperature is an indication whether the adjacent cell will enter TR based on the modelled critical self-heating temperature limit. Once the adjacent cell top temperature exceeds the critical temperature, the self-heating due to solid electrolyte interphase decomposition would be triggered. CFD simulations have also been conducted for an open cluster as shown in Fig. 15. The central LIB was assumed to undergo TR and represented by the circular patch of 10 mm in diameter on the central LIB top as inlet. The predicted temperature on the top of the adjacent LIBs with the two different radiative heat transfer treatments are shown in Fig. 16. The results indicate that the TR and resulting fire would not trigger TR propagation in an open cluster, i.e. without casing. However, it should be noted that the numerical simulations here did not consider LIB side rupture and other electric connections, which may affect the TR propagation dynamics. Even in the absence of LIB side rupture, the influencing factors will be very different in enclosed/encased LIB clusters. In such situations, the ejected hot gases/sparks and the fire will impinge on the casing and spread into the narrow gaps, leading to enhanced transfer of heat energy to the adjacent ones as well as those further away (Chander and Ray, 2005; Lopez et al., 2015). Work is ongoing to use the developed modelling approaches to analyse TR propagation in enclosed LIB clusters. In terms of LIB safety, it is also worth consider the fire hazards in conjunction with the integrity of LIB modules, etc. For example, whether it is viable to use weak casing for some applications so the fire can easily penetrate rather than spreading inside the module to trigger TR in the neighbouring cells, leading to much large fires.

Numerical and experimental studies have been conducted for high energy density 4.8 Ah 21700 cylindrical NMC LIBs at 100% SOC to characterise the TR evolution and resulting fire. The key findings can be summarised as follows:
–
The LIB was characterised under thermal abuse conditions in EV-ARC and cone calorimetry tests. The mean peak values of the heat release rate, cell surface and internal temperatures were experimentally found to be 3.6 kW, 753 °C and 1080 °C, respectively.
–
An analytical model has been developed to predict the cell internal pressure from vent opening onwards. The model uses the measured cell internal temperature, canister pressure and overall gas generation in EV-ARC tests as input data. The predicted cell internal pressure profile following venting is inline with literature findings for similar cell types. The calculated internal pressure had a peak of value 4.56 bar during TR.
–
The predicted cell internal pressure and measured temperature along with the mean gas compositions were used as boundary conditions for the subsequent 3-D CFD simulations of the TR induced fire. The predicted heat release rate achieved reasonably good agreement with the cone calorimeter measurements.
–
Predictions were also conducted for open cell clusters, revealing that TR is unlikely to propagate to the neighbouring LIBs in an open cluster in the absence of LIB side rupture. It should, however, be noted that the situation would be different for enclosed clusters and with electric connections, for which the impingement of the ejected hot gases/sparks on the casing and their direct contact with other LIBs will augment the impact of the thermal hazards.

The present CFD modelling approach can serve as a useful tool to assess the thermal and environment hazards due to LIB fires and aid design optimisation of mitigation measures in practical enclosed LIB clusters/modules.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work is conducted within the frame of the “Lithium-Ion Battery Research In Safety (LIBRIS)” project funded by Innovate UK (Project no. 105296). The authors are thankful to colleagues Martin Dawson, Jonathan Sansom and Alex Czernianin at WMG who helped to instrument the LIBs for internal temperature and pressure measurements.

Aspects of the work described in this paper were undertaken at the Health and Safety Executive (HSE) Science and Research Centre. Its contents, including any opinions and/or conclusions expressed, are those of the authors alone and do not necessarily reflect HSE policy.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Wang et al., 2021

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Experimental study on the cell-jet temperatures of abused prismatic Ni-rich automotive batteries under medium and high states of charge
Appl. Therm. Eng.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Modeling of thermal runaway propagation of NMC battery packs after fast charging operation
